selective_codes.code_ja: 知識領域独占対多元主義軸
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous knowledge systems organize themselves along a fundamental axis between monopolistic specialization—where a single resource domain commands near-total epistemic investment—and pluralistic integration, where multiple subsistence knowledge domains are maintained in balanced or asymmetric complementarity. Monopolistic configurations emerge under ecological forcing conditions that reward deep specialization and impose prohibitive switching costs, while pluralistic configurations emerge where ecological diversity, resource unpredictability, or risk-spreading imperatives make cross-domain competence adaptive. The position of a community's knowledge system on this axis structures which knowledge types are elaborated, transmitted with high fidelity, and accorded social prestige.

selective_codes.code_ja: 生態帯固有の知識専門化シグネチャ
selective_codes.theory: Each major ecological zone generates a distinctive knowledge specialization signature—a characteristic configuration of dominant knowledge domains, transmission structures, and social valuation patterns that reflects the biome's resource composition, seasonal rhythms, and productivity constraints. Arctic and boreal zones produce hunting-dominant monopolistic systems; temperate and coastal interfaces produce integrated dual-resource systems; arid lands produce dispersed multi-species botanical expertise; tropical forests produce cultivar-centered swidden knowledge; and maritime islands produce sea-garden integrated architectures. These ecological signatures are not deterministic endpoints but probabilistic attractors around which cultural variation clusters, with communities' specific knowledge configurations representing culturally mediated positions within ecologically constrained possibility spaces.

selective_codes.code_ja: 生業生態学的知識階層決定構造
selective_codes.theory: The ecological productivity and compositional character of a community's biome is the primary determinant of which subsistence knowledge domain achieves epistemic dominance, with extreme environments—arctic tundra, boreal forest, open ocean—producing near-monopolistic knowledge specialization while intermediate-productivity environments such as savannas, temperate woodlands, and coastal interfaces generate pluralistic knowledge architectures. This ecological determinism is not absolute but sets the parameters within which cultural investment, historical trajectory, and social organization select among viable knowledge configurations. The gradient from forager-primary to agriculture-dominant systems is thus fundamentally an ecological gradient that cultural processes subsequently elaborate, contest, and symbolically transform.

selective_codes.code_ja: 居住・親族的知識伝達経路の構造設計
selective_codes.theory: Post-marital residence rules and kinship reckoning principles jointly constitute the architectural infrastructure through which craft and productive knowledge flows across generations, with patrilocal-patrilineal configurations concentrating male craft knowledge through co-resident agnatic apprenticeship, matrilocal-matrilineal configurations enabling female craft knowledge aggregation in shared domestic spaces, and flexible bilateral or ambilocal arrangements producing individualized, network-mediated transmission pathways. The structural coupling or decoupling of residence and descent principles is the critical variable: when residence and descent align, knowledge transmission achieves high fidelity and spatial concentration; when they conflict—as in virilocal matrilineal or matrikin dispersal configurations—transmission chains fracture and knowledge risks attrition. This architectural principle operates across all subsistence types and ecological zones as a universal but variably instantiated mechanism.

selective_codes.code_ja: 多様性維持による知識体系のレジリエンス
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous knowledge systems that maintain multiple active subsistence knowledge domains—through mixed economies, flexible kinship structures, or institutionalized complementarity between specialized knowledge fields—demonstrate greater adaptive resilience than highly specialized monopolistic systems, because cross-domain knowledge provides fallback capacity when primary subsistence strategies fail due to ecological perturbation, political disruption, or resource depletion. The mixed subsistence knowledge architectures found in intermediate-productivity ecological zones and flexible bilateral kinship systems are not merely transitional stages between foraging and agricultural specialization but represent distinct adaptive strategies that preserve knowledge diversity as an insurance mechanism. Communities that allow dominant knowledge domains to competitively displace alternative expertise are thus trading long-term adaptive resilience for short-term productive efficiency.

selective_codes.code_ja: 知識伝達忠実度の空間的近接性依存構造
selective_codes.theory: The fidelity, completeness, and elaboration of knowledge transmission in indigenous systems is fundamentally dependent on the spatial proximity between knowledge holders and learners over sustained periods, with co-resident kin networks producing the highest-fidelity transmission through embedded daily apprenticeship, observation, and co-practice, while spatially dispersed transmission networks produce attenuated, simplified, or selectively retained knowledge configurations. Kinship rules that maintain knowledge practitioners in shared residential units—through patrilocal, matrilocal, or avunculocal arrangements—thus function as spatial solutions to the cognitive and social problem of knowledge transfer across generations, converting abstract descent-based authority into concrete co-present learning relationships. When residence patterns undermine this spatial proximity—through virilocal dispersal of female craft specialists or matrikin scattering in patrilocal systems—knowledge transmission chains develop structural weaknesses that accumulate into systemic knowledge loss across generations.

selective_codes.code_ja: 知識集積・分散の構造的緊張
selective_codes.theory: All indigenous knowledge systems operate under a fundamental structural tension between forces that concentrate expertise in bounded social units—enabling high-fidelity transmission, elaboration, and quality control—and forces that disperse knowledge across social space through marriage, migration, and flexible residence, enabling knowledge sharing, innovation through cross-pollination, and community-wide resilience. The resolution of this tension is not uniform but reflects the interaction of kinship rules, marriage patterns, ecological conditions, and cultural values about knowledge ownership and accessibility. Configurations that achieve high concentration—through patrikin or matrikin residential aggregation—gain transmission depth at the cost of knowledge insularity, while dispersed configurations gain breadth at the cost of fidelity.

selective_codes.code_ja: 生業知識集約化の変遷軌跡
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous knowledge systems undergo directional intensification trajectories in which investment accumulates in dominant subsistence domains through positive feedback mechanisms—agricultural communities invest more in agronomy, which increases yields, which justifies further investment, which progressively marginalizes alternative knowledge domains—producing historically contingent but structurally stable knowledge configurations that are difficult to reverse. The trajectory from mixed forager systems through mixed economies toward agricultural or pastoral dominance is not ecologically determined alone but is driven by intergenerational knowledge investment decisions that create path dependencies, with each generation inheriting a knowledge portfolio that makes continued investment in dominant domains more rational than diversification. Communities at the high-intensification end exhibit near-exclusive knowledge concentration and corresponding vulnerability to the failure of the dominant subsistence system.

selective_codes.code_ja: 文化的アイデンティティへの知識の象徴的埋め込み
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous knowledge systems are not neutral repositories of adaptive information but are symbolically embedded in community identity, cosmological frameworks, and prestige economies in ways that elevate certain knowledge domains to identity-constitutive status regardless of their functional-economic importance, and that suppress other domains to marginal or vestigial roles regardless of their practical utility. This symbolic embedding creates knowledge configurations that cannot be explained by ecological or economic optimization alone—forager communities may maintain elaborate botanical knowledge as identity markers long after agricultural transition renders it economically marginal, while pastoral communities may symbolically elaborate herding knowledge into cosmological systems that make it structurally resistant to displacement. The decoupling of knowledge status from subsistence contribution thus reveals indigenous knowledge systems as simultaneously adaptive ecological archives and culturally curated identity projects.

selective_codes.code_ja: ジェンダーによる知識領域境界形成機制
selective_codes.theory: Gender functions as a primary boundary-marking mechanism in indigenous knowledge systems, organizing not only the division of productive labor but the social topology of knowledge domains themselves—defining which bodies can hold, transmit, and authorize specific forms of expertise. Gendered knowledge localization creates semi-autonomous epistemic territories within communities, with distinct transmission pathways, spatial contexts, and social valuations operating simultaneously for male-coded and female-coded knowledge domains. The interaction between gender boundaries and kinship-residence structures produces the specific transmission vulnerabilities of each system: virilocal societies fracture female craft lineages through affinal relocation, while matrilineal authority paradoxes arise when male avuncular authority governs inherently female-gendered knowledge domains.

selective_codes.code_ja: 野生資源知識の地位と生業貢献の分離構造
selective_codes.theory: The social and epistemological status assigned to wild resource knowledge is systematically decoupled from its quantitative contribution to subsistence, reflecting a community's prestige economy, symbolic identity investments, and historical memory of subsistence transitions rather than purely functional assessments of economic importance. Communities that have undergone agricultural intensification frequently marginalize gathering knowledge symbolically even when it retains meaningful caloric contribution, while other communities elevate wild resource expertise to identity-constitutive status that exceeds its material role—embedding it in ritual, cosmology, and social prestige systems. This decoupling reveals that indigenous knowledge systems are not merely adaptive information repositories but are culturally curated archives in which symbolic valuation actively shapes what is transmitted, elaborated, and remembered.

selective_codes.code_ja: 知識権威・系譜・ジェンダーの矛盾構造
selective_codes.theory: In many indigenous knowledge systems, formal authority over knowledge transmission is held by social actors who are structurally or genealogically positioned to control succession rather than those who are practically most competent in the knowledge domain, producing systematic paradoxes in which authority and expertise are misaligned along gender or descent lines. The matrilineal male authority paradox—where maternal uncles control female-gendered craft knowledge—exemplifies a broader pattern in which descent-based authority structures overlay and sometimes override practice-based expertise hierarchies, creating internal tensions that shape both what knowledge is transmitted and how authentically it is transmitted across generations. These authority-expertise misalignments constitute a structural source of knowledge transformation and potential erosion in indigenous systems undergoing social change.

selective_codes.code_ja: 婚姻移動による知識移転の普遍的機制
selective_codes.theory: Marriage-induced residential relocation constitutes a universal but structurally differentiated mechanism of inter-community knowledge transfer in indigenous societies, functioning simultaneously as a knowledge acquisition pathway for relocating individuals—who must learn new craft and ecological knowledge in their new residential community—and as a knowledge dispersal vector that carries expertise from natal to affinal communities. The direction, completeness, and social recognition of this knowledge transfer depends critically on whether the relocating individual enters a knowledge-rich or knowledge-poor environment relative to their natal community, and on whether the receiving community's descent and gender ideology facilitates or suppresses incorporation of external knowledge. Affinal knowledge acquisition thus represents a structural feature of all exogamous kinship systems that has profound consequences for the long-term distribution of specialized knowledge across indigenous landscapes.

selective_codes.code_ja: 水域・陸域知識統合の必然的要請
selective_codes.theory: Communities situated at the interface of productive aquatic and terrestrial ecological systems—coastal, riverine, and island environments—face a structural imperative to develop and maintain integrated knowledge systems that bridge marine or freshwater and land-based domains, producing distinctive hybrid expertise architectures that cannot be reduced to either aquatic or terrestrial specialization alone. The degree of integration versus compartmentalization between aquatic and terrestrial knowledge reflects the degree to which ecological systems are seasonally complementary versus temporally competitive, with high complementarity enabling the most elaborated integrative knowledge configurations. These aquatic-terrestrial knowledge systems represent some of the most complex indigenous knowledge architectures precisely because they must encode the interaction dynamics of two distinct ecological logics.

selective_codes.code_ja: 民族誌的記録偏向による知識の不可視化
selective_codes.theory: Systematic biases in ethnographic documentation—favoring male-held, hunting-related, ritual-adjacent, and prestige-marked knowledge—have produced a structurally distorted record of indigenous traditional knowledge in which women's gathering knowledge, non-prestige subsistence activities, and knowledge domains that do not map onto Western categorical hierarchies are chronically underrepresented or entirely absent. These documentation gaps are not random but are patterned by the same social hierarchies that structure knowledge status within communities, meaning that the database reflects a double marginalization: knowledge that is socially marginal within communities is also epistemologically marginalized in the ethnographic record. Grounded theory built on this dataset must therefore treat absences as analytically significant findings about the social politics of knowledge recognition rather than as neutral data lacunae.

samples.title: Settlement patterns: Segmented communities, i.e., those divided into barrios, wards, or hamlets, each of which is essentially a localized kin group, a clan or ramage, in the absence of any indication of local exogamy. Large extended families (see "Domestic organization"), are treated as clan-barrios if they are integrated by a rule of ambilineal, matrilineal, or patrilineal descent.

data_sources.name: D-PLACE (Database of Places, Language, Culture and Environment)
data_sources.ethical_notes: Academic ethnographic data. Compiled from published sources with varying consent histories.

data_sources.name: UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage
data_sources.ethical_notes: Fully public. All elements inscribed with community consent (FPIC compliant). Safest source.

data_sources.name: Standard Cross-Cultural Sample (SCCS)
data_sources.ethical_notes: Academic data from Murdock & White (1969). 186 representative societies, ~2,000 variables.

data_sources.name: Binford Hunter-Gatherer Dataset
data_sources.ethical_notes: Compiled by Lewis Binford from ethnographic literature. Focus on subsistence ecology.

data_sources.name: Ethnographic Atlas
data_sources.ethical_notes: George P. Murdock (1967). 1,291 societies. Integrated into D-PLACE.
